pattern: Starting from the baseline where one mouse eats everything, switching any single item to the other mouse changes the total by a fixed, independent amount, so the best set of exactly k switches is simply the k items with the largest such gain.
hints: Imagine the second mouse eats every piece of cheese by default, then think about what happens to the running total when you switch exactly one piece over to the first mouse instead.
Switching piece i changes the total by reward1[i] - reward2[i], and that change does not depend on which other pieces you have already switched -- the swaps are independent of each other.
Since each swap's gain is independent, the optimal choice of exactly k swaps is the k pieces with the largest reward1[i] - reward2[i] value, so sort the differences and take the top k.

edge_inputs.description: k = 0, the second mouse eats every piece
k equal to n, the first mouse eats every piece
reward1 and reward2 identical everywhere, so the total is fixed regardless of which k are chosen
k forces switching pieces even where reward2 is far larger than reward1, since k is an exact count not a cap
several pieces tie on the exact same reward1 - reward2 gap
gaps of the same magnitude but opposite sign tie for the single switch

stress.description: random reward arrays with a random exact switch count k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 5000))
    reward1 = [rng.randint(1, 1000) for _ in range(n)]
    reward2 = [rng.randint(1, 1000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, n)
    return [reward1, reward2, k]
